pages.raw_text:
As was forecasted In this news
paper q few weeks ago, the strife
among the electrical workers and
the action of the American Federal
ton of Labor executive council IN
taking sides with the minority fac
ton and seeking to foist discredited
officials upon the majority, have
thrown the whole labor movement
into turmoil.


A score OF state and city centra.
bodies have had their charters re-
voked or have openly defied the
federation officials to outlaw them,
and circulars and newspaper state
ments are being issued daily in ex-
PLANTATION of some phase of the
fight. 1t is now freely predicted
by some of the most conservative
labor officials that II the Toronto
convention of the A. F. Of l.. next
month does not propose plan to
speedily adjust the trouble big
split Is likely to occur, and another
national federation may be
launched.


The factional differences among
the electrical workers, which are
developing some ugly charges, are
being bvershadowed by important
principles and interpretations Of
trade union laws and policies that
are creating much discussion. Have
the American Federation of Labor
officials the right to interfere In
the internal affairs Of affiliated or
ganizations? That Is the question
that Is being asked on every hand.


The A. F. OF l.. constitution says
no--that affiliated bodies are
granted absolute autonomy in
managing their own business. On
the other hand, the executive coun.
Gil claims the right to act as ar.
bitrator and to discipline constitu.
. ent bodies or fractions thereof. II
the latter contention Is upheld, the
! autonomists declare, then the prin-
ciple or democracy is subverted to
the usurpations of autocracy, the
rock upon which the knights of
Labor was wrecked, and dismem-
berment will surely be the fate Of
the A. F. OF L.


It Is charged that National Civic
! Federation influences have been In
jected into the present controversy.
The acceptance on the part of Pres
ident McNulty- of the electrical
workers of an invitation extended
to a number Of labor men by the
Civic Federation to investigate mu-
nicipal ownership in Europe sew
/ eral years ago was the beginning
of the present trouble. The cleo
trical workers are no friends of the
c. F.. and Belmont and Carnegie
were denounced as bride givers in
paying all expenses of the labor
men selected for the European
jaunt, while McNulty was also
sharply criticised for collecting his
salary while abroad and unable to
transact union business.


Then charges followed in rapid
succession-charge that McNulty
was endeavoring to compel the
withdrawal of the unions $4,000
from Cleveland banks paying per
cent interest and deposit the same
In bank at Springfield, ILL.,, offer
ing but 2 per cent; further charges
that he presented the New York
local union with large amount of
money and was In turn presented
with large diamond; still further
charges of padded expense ac-
counts, malfeasance, discrimina-
ton and incompetency.


A big majority of the members
called for special convention at
st. Louis, In September last year.
which McNulty attempted to, pre-
